column3: Be cautious if you have known allergies to plants in the Asteraceae family, as Yarrow belongs to this family and may trigger allergic reactions.

Yarrow can have a mild blood-thinning effect, so if you have a blood clotting disorder or are taking anticoagulant medications, consult a healthcare professional before using Yarrow.
Avoid excessive consumption of Yarrow, as it may cause adverse effects. Follow recommended dosage guidelines.
Do not use Yarrow continuously for an extended period without the guidance of a qualified herbalist or healthcare provider.

Ensure you obtain Yarrow from a reputable source to ensure its purity and avoid potential contaminants.
If you have underlying medical conditions, such as liver or kidney issues, consult with a healthcare professional before using Yarrow as a medicinal herb.
If making your own Yarrow preparations, ensure you have the proper knowledge of dosage and preparation methods to avoid misuse or potential harm.
Be aware of potential side effects, which can include digestive upset, dizziness, and increased skin sensitivity, and discontinue use if these occur.

column2: Discontinue in case of adverse effects

column2: Not a substitute for professional advice
column3: Sweet flag should not be used as a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always seek guidance from a qualified healthcare practitioner.

Seek advice from a qualified healthcare practitioner before using horse chestnut as a medicinal remedy, especially if you have pre-existing medical conditions or are taking other medications.
Follow recommended dosage guidelines carefully, as improper doses may lead to adverse effects. Excessive consumption can result in toxicity and pose health risks.
Never consume raw horse chestnut as it contains esculin, a compound that can be toxic. Only use processed and standardized forms available in supplements or extracts.
Be cautious if you have allergies to horse chestnut or related plants. Allergic reactions may include skin irritation, itching, or respiratory issues.
Pregnant or breastfeeding women should avoid using horse chestnut, as its safety in these situations has not been conclusively established.
Individuals with bleeding disorders or those taking anticoagulant medications should use horse chestnut cautiously, as it may increase the risk of bleeding.
Regularly monitor liver function if using horse chestnut, as some cases of liver damage have been reported. Discontinue use if symptoms such as jaundice or abdominal pain occur.
Monitor blood sugar levels closely if you have diabetes, as horse chestnut may affect insulin sensitivity. Adjustments to diabetes medications may be necessary.

Be aware of potential interactions with medications, particularly anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, and drugs metabolized by the liver. Consult with a healthcare professional to avoid adverse reactions.

Avoid long-term use of horse chestnut without medical supervision. Prolonged usage may lead to side effects, and intermittent breaks should be considered to assess tolerance and minimize potential risks.

Pregnant and breastfeeding individuals should exercise caution with garlic supplements and consult their healthcare provider to ensure its safety during these periods.
